The meals-per-day item is explicit in 1994b (Part III Sec.5 Q1); the 1997 Consumption-Habits section appears to drop the explicit meals-per-day question while retaining shared-stock / away-eating items. The 1989 pre-expansion instrument differs and was not separately page-verified for these items.

notes:
EthiopiaRHS is the IFPRI/AAU/CSAE Ethiopian Rural Household Survey (ERHS), a purposive 15-village rural panel — NOT a World Bank LSMS-ISA survey and NOT in the microdata.worldbank.org catalog. It is on the Harvard/IFPRI Dataverse (doi:10.7910/DVN/T8G8IV). The library treats it as a distinct module from the WB Ethiopia ESS/LSMS-ISA. I did not need WebSearch/WebFetch: the full, authoritative household questionnaires are DVC-materialized in-repo and I read them page-by-page (1994b Round 2: 40 pp; 1997 Round 4: ~148 PDF pages incl. code sheets). Determinations are grounded in those fetched primary instruments rather than external web sources.

(A) SUBJECTIVE WELL-BEING: ABSENT (high confidence). No life-satisfaction, Cantril-ladder, happiness, or life-evaluation item in any wave's instrument. The only subjective-perception items are (i) retrospective relative-wealth ratings of parents at time of marriage (1997 Part IV Sec.5) and (ii) a general-knowledge 'Quiz' (1997 Sec.15) — neither is a well-being self-report.

(B) FOOD SECURITY: PRESENT but bespoke (medium confidence) — there is NO validated scale (no FIES/HFIAS/HHS/FCS/rCSI/months-of-inadequate-provisioning). What exists: a meals-per-day food-adequacy question (1994b Part III Sec.5 Q1), idiosyncratic vs. covariate bad-harvest coping/support questions (1994b Part II Sec.8 Q64/Q65), and detailed crop/livestock weather-and-pest shock batteries with moderate/severe severity (both 1994b and 1997). Confidence is medium because these are scattered bespoke items, not a labeled food-security module, and the exact item set varies by round (e.g., the explicit meals-per-day question is in 1994b but appears dropped in the 1997 Consumption-Habits section).

Caveat on waves: detailed page-level verification was done on the 1994b (R2) and 1997 (R4) household questionnaires. 1994a (R1) and 1995 (R3) share the same survey program/instrument family and the per-wave SOURCE.org files point to the same Dataverse deposit; the 1989 pre-expansion instrument (ERHS_hhquestionnaire_1989.pdf, also in-repo) differs structurally and was not page-verified for these specific items. Library-side wired waves: 1989, 1994a, 1994b, 1995, 1997 (1999/2004/2009 exist as Documentation but are NOT wired — only pre-aggregated consumption, per _/CONTENTS.org).

Sec.5 Family History II, Sec.15 Quiz, Sec.16 Domestic Situation) and Part V Community Work/Public Works. Whole-instrument keyword scan (satisf/happ/cantril/ladder/well-being/life-evaluation) returned zero genuine SWB hits across both full questionnaires.

Food security (present, bespoke) CONFIRMED verbatim: 1994b Part III Sec.5 Q1 'How many meals PER DAY did your household eat in the last week?'; Part II Sec.8 Q64-65 bad-harvest support; Sub-section 9.2 'Events during the last Meher season'; Section 10 'Events during the Belg season'. 1997 Part II Sec.8 'Events during the last Kiremt season' Q1-9; Sec.9 Belg; Sec.5A Q11 'Did any of your livestock suffer from any of the following factors in the last year?'.

The 1997 Part I-V TOC does appear at extracted page 5. Substantive structural and section claims are fully supported.

notes:

FOOD SECURITY (high confidence): Section 8A is the standard FIES (Food Insecurity Experience Scale) 8-item battery — the eight canonical items (WORRIED, HEALTHY, FEWFOODS, SKIPPED, ATELESS, RANOUT, HUNGRY, WHLDAY) appear verbatim as s08aq01–s08aq08, with 12-month reference period and frequency-probe follow-ups (s08aq07a, s08aq08a). SUBJECTIVE WELL-BEING (medium confidence): Section 20 ('Pauvreté relative' in 2018-19; 'Subjective poverty' in 2021-22) contains subjective self-assessment items — notably s20q05, self-placement of the household on a poor-to-rich wellbeing ladder ('échelle de bien-être pauvre à riche'), plus comparative living-standard ratings (vs neighbors, vs capital) and a subjective minimum-income question. If the rubric requires life-evaluation/happiness specifically, this is a near-miss (subjective economic wellbeing ladder only); hence medium confidence on the SWB classification.

Subjective-poverty items present include perceptions of national poverty (s20q01), self-assessed living standard relative to income (s20q02), comparisons to neighbors (s20q03) and capital-city residents (s20q04), household well-being self-ranking on a ladder (s20q05), and minimum income needed for a decent living (s20q06). This directly evidences the claimed Section 20 subjective-poverty module.

Food security / FIES (confirmed): The cited URL for file s08a_me_sen2018 describes "Section 8A: Food security," with the canonical 8-item FIES battery over the preceding 12 months — worried about food (s08aq01), unable to eat healthy/nutritious food (s08aq02), monotonous diet (s08aq03), skipped meals (s08aq04), ate less than appropriate (s08aq05), ran out of food (s08aq06), hungry but did not eat (s08aq07), and went a whole day without eating (s08aq08), plus frequency follow-ups (s08aq07a, s08aq08a). This matches the FIES instrument exactly. Note: the page does not literally print the string "FIES," but the item set is unambiguously the Food Insecurity Experience Scale.

Minor note: the claim references the 2021-22 wave (catalog 6278) files s20a_me_sen2021 etc., which were not independently fetched; verification was performed on the 2018-19 citations actually provided, both of which hold.

Survey identification is solid: repo SOURCE.org DOIs resolve to WB catalog SCG_2002_LSMS_v01_M (catalog 80) and SCG_2003_LSMS_v01_M (catalog 81). (A) SWB: There is NO life-satisfaction / happiness / Cantril-ladder / general life-evaluation module. What IS present is a SUBJECTIVE ECONOMIC well-being item: a 5-point self-rated household financial-standing question and a subjective 'minimum funds for basic needs' question, both documented and tabulated (Table 1.4, section 1.3 'Subjective poverty') in the official Statistical Office of Serbia LSMS 2002-2007 report, which I fetched and extracted (285pp PDF). The self-assessed-health graphs (report section 7) are health-perception, not life-evaluation, so not counted as SWB.

(B) Food security: marked ABSENT at MEDIUM (not high) confidence. The full 10-module questionnaire structure was fetched for both waves and contains no experience-based food-insecurity/coping battery; food coverage is a 7-day expenditure diary + recall consumption. Confidence is medium rather than high because absence is inferred from the module list rather than a line-by-line reading of every questionnaire variable.

Caveat: this is an older (2002/2003), non-LSMS-ISA transition-economy survey. SWB wave attribution: 2002 directly confirmed; 2003 (panel Wave 2, identical module structure) inferred. The Roma Settlement Survey (2003) and MOP/Family Income Support (2002) are parallel sub-samples using the same instrument.

variables: Q1 household life-satisfaction item: "Taking everything into account, how satisfied is this household with the way it lives these days?" with a 5-point response scale: Very satisfied(-1) / Satisfied(-2) / Neither satisfied nor dissatisfied(-3) / Dissatisfied(-4) / Very dissatisfied(-5)

A full-text scan of the household questionnaire found no FIES/HFIAS/HHS/FCS/rCSI/coping items and no wording for hunger, going without food, skipping/reducing meals, food running out, or food being inadequate/insufficient.

food_security.citation: https://www.datafirst.uct.ac.za/dataportal/index.php/catalog/820/download/11093 (PSLSD 1993 Household Questionnaire PDF, Section 3 'Food Spending and Consumption' is purely expenditure/quantity; no food-adequacy or hunger item found anywhere in the 57-page instrument)

notes:
Survey identity: the LSMS-library "South Africa 1993" maps (via DOI 10.48529/9567-t539, which 302-redirects to the WB study ZAF_1993_IHS_v01_M) to the World Bank-sponsored LSMS known as the Project for Statistics on Living Standards and Development (PSLSD) 1993, conducted by SALDRU/UCT (~9,000 households, fielded Aug 1993–early 1994 ahead of the April 1994 election). WB catalog ID #902; also mirrored at DataFirst catalog #820 (merged version). This is a genuine LSMS, not an LSMS-ISA wave.

SWB = PRESENT (high confidence): verified against the actual fetched household questionnaire PDF. Section 9 "Perceived Quality of Life" leads with a household-level life-satisfaction item on a standard 5-point Very-satisfied-to-Very-dissatisfied scale. SALDRU is widely credited (Development Southern Africa, 1996) with pioneering this household satisfaction measure in PSLSD. The corresponding repo data file is S5_PQL.dta (WB-labeled "9 Perceived quality of life"); note the LSMS-library data_scheme.yml for South Africa does NOT currently expose a SWB feature — it only declares sample, cluster_features, household_roster, individual_education — so the satisfaction data is in the raw source but not yet harmonized into a library table.

Food security = ABSENT (high confidence): I read the full questionnaire (57 pages, ~100k chars extracted). Section 3 "Food Spending and Consumption" is a conventional item-by-item expenditure + own-production + in-kind battery with no experiential food-insecurity, hunger, or adequacy question. No standard instrument (FIES/HFIAS/HHS/FCS/rCSI/CSI/months-of-inadequate-provisioning) is present, and there is no bespoke food-insufficiency item. Caveat: 'absent' here is well-grounded because the fetched questionnaire clearly covers the food domain (so the area was in scope) yet contains no insecurity item. Food-quantity/expenditure data ARE rich and the library does derive food_expenditures/food_quantities/food_prices for this country, but that is consumption measurement, not food-security measurement.

Verification limits: I could not pull the underlying .dta files locally (DVC remote/S3 fetch failed in this environment), so variable-level confirmation rests on (a) the fetched DataFirst household-questionnaire PDF and (b) the World Bank catalog data-dictionary file labels — both primary/authoritative. WebFetch's small-model summary of the WB catalog initially missed Section 9, but direct PDF text extraction shows the exact wording quoted above.

notes:
Survey is the Tajikistan Living Standards Survey (TLSS), a World Bank LSMS series (not LSMS-ISA). DOIs in repo SOURCE.org resolve to WB Microdata: 1999=10.48529/hjw2-fg61 (catalog 279), 2003=10.48529/v9gt-4x62 (catalog 278), 2007=10.48529/39ay-kr43 (catalog 72), 2009=10.48529/frwh-7q04 (catalog 73). 2007 and 2009 form a panel (1,500 of 4,860 2007 HHs re-interviewed in 2009).

Both modules are confirmed at the VARIABLE level for 2007 and 2009 by reading the actual repo .dta variable labels (via DVCFileSystem + pyreadstat), corroborated by fetched WB catalog study-description pages. The 2009 m8a module is a single combined block containing both an overall life-satisfaction question (M8AQ9) AND the complete 9-item HFIAS battery (M8AQ11-M8AQ29) AND a 13-month food-availability calendar (M8AQ34); 2009 m8b is a dedicated 26-item coping-strategies index. The 2007 module 9 (r1m9/sm9) adds a 6-step self-rated economic ladder (m9aq9a/b) alongside the life-satisfaction item and HFIAS battery; round 2 (r2m9) repeats the food-security battery with a 2-week recall.

Caveats: (1) 1999 SSEC8 sec 8C carries a bespoke subjective-finances + food-coping battery (s8cq1-9) but NO overall life-satisfaction/Cantril item and NO formal HFIAS — its food module is a coping-strategies/perceived-situation battery. (2) 2003 module 8 is confirmed by the WB catalog to be 'Subjective poverty and food security', but the repo's module8.dta has all variable labels stripped (None), so item-level SWB/HFIAS wording could not be verified from source; treated as present-by-catalog with item content unconfirmed. The 2007 'Subjective Beliefs' module (r1m14/sm14) uses self-anchoring vignette ladders (a recognized subjective-welfare instrument) including a food/meat-consumption vignette ladder.

The library's data_scheme.yml for Tajikistan currently wires only interview_date, cluster_features, household_roster, individual_education, and sample — neither the SWB nor the food-security modules are currently harmonized as library features, but the raw source data is present in-repo (under DVC) for all four waves.

SWB (confirmed): The 2009 catalog (catalog/73) lists a verbatim module "Subjective Poverty and Food Security" and a separate "Coping Strategies" module. The 2003 catalog (catalog/278) confirms module 8 = "Subjective poverty and food security". The 2007 catalog (catalog/72) confirms both a "Subjective Poverty and Food Security" module and a "Subjective Beliefs" module with vignette rankings on a 1-6 scale. In-repo variable labels corroborate: 2009 m8a.dta has subjective-poverty items (M8AQ2 financial satisfaction, M8AQ5 minimal income, M8AQ9 life satisfaction); 2007 r1m9.dta has the same plus a self-anchoring ladder (m9aq9a/b, "6-step ladder"); 2007 r1m14.dta has self-anchoring vignette ladders (m14bq2 "on a scale of 1-6", m14bq3a-e and m14bq4a-f "Family A/B/C/D ... indicate on which step you and your family would be found"). All claimed file paths exist in the repo (m8a/m8b/m9 for 2009; r1m9/r2m9/sm9 and r1m14/sm14 for 2007; module8 for 2003; SSEC8 for 1999).

Food security (confirmed): The 2007 catalog explicitly states "information needed to generate the Household Food Insecurity Access Scale (HFIAS)". In-repo labels confirm the full 9-item HFIAS battery with frequency follow-ups: 2009 m8a M8AQ11-M8AQ29; 2007 r1m9 m9aq14-m9aq32. Months-of-inadequate-food-provisioning is present (2009 M8AQ34_1..13 monthly food availability Oct2008-Oct2009; 2007 m9aq33a..m). A separate coping-strategies module is confirmed: 2009 m8b.dta lists 26 distinct coping actions (M8BQ2_1..26), and 2009 catalog lists "Coping Strategies" as its own module.

Corrections / caveats (do not change the verdicts): (1) The instrument claim of "HFIAS (2007, 2009)" is well supported; HFIAS is named in the 2007 catalog and the question battery matches the standard 9 HFIAS items in both waves. The 2009 catalog does NOT name HFIAS, but the m8a variable labels are the same HFIAS battery, so the in-repo evidence carries it. (2) WEAK EVIDENCE for 1999: the 1999 SSEC8.DTA file exists, but all its substantive variable labels are blank/None in the source (only s8aq825 "total household expenditure" is labeled). The in-repo labels therefore do NOT independently substantiate "subjective poverty/food security" content for 1999 SSEC8 section 8C; presence rests on file existence and the codebook/catalog (catalog/279, not fetched here). This is a minor gap; the SWB and food-security verdicts are driven by the strongly-confirmed 2003/2007/2009 evidence. (3) The "Subjective Beliefs" 2007 vignette module is more precisely about pensions/social-security expectations (m14a) and remittance/quality-of-life self-anchoring vignettes (m14b) rather than poverty per se, but it does use the self-anchoring vignette ladder method as claimed.

Survey identification: This is the World Bank LSMS-ISA Uganda National Panel Survey (UNPS), an ISA panel, NOT a one-off LSMS. FOOD SECURITY (present, high confidence): The instrument is a BESPOKE UNPS battery, not a standardized scale. It is NOT FIES, HFIAS, HHS, FCS, or rCSI. Its hallmark is (a) a single binary 'did not have enough food to feed the household' question (h17q09/s17q09), (b) a 12-month calendar of which months food was inadequate (s17q10A-L) = a months-of-inadequate-food-provisioning instrument, and (c) a reasons grid (s17q11A-X). Accompanied by meal-frequency, ran-out-of-salt coping, and child-breakfast items. Separately, GSEC16 'Shocks and Coping Strategies' (every wave) records up to 3 household coping strategies per shock and whether shocks reduced food production/purchases. In 2005-06 the food-deprivation battery lives in GSEC15 (GSEC17 that year is a LAND module, not food); the welfare/food module migrated to GSEC17 from 2009-10 on.

SWB (absent, high confidence): No life-satisfaction, Cantril ladder, happiness, self-assessed welfare, or self-ranked wealth-group question exists. The module sometimes called 'Welfare' is objective material-deprivation (clothes/blanket/shoes), not subjective evaluation. Verified by reading every HH-questionnaire module label for 2019-20 and regex-scanning for SWB keywords (zero genuine hits).

Caveat: I confirmed variable wording directly from the repo's source .dta metadata for the waves listed; 2018-19 food-security file was not individually opened (its GSEC17 file was not present in that wave's listing under the same name), but the WB catalog 3795 documents the same UNPS structure, and the module is continuous across all surrounding waves, so present is the well-supported inference there.
